front: Create a namespace called `mynamespace`
back:
```bash
kubectl create namespace mynamespace
```

Creates a new namespace imperatively. Namespaces isolate groups of resources within a single cluster. Short alias: `kubectl create ns mynamespace`.

[Namespaces](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/namespaces/)

front: Create a pod named `nginx` with image `nginx` in namespace `mynamespace`
back:
```bash
kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --restart=Never -n mynamespace
```

`kubectl run` creates a single pod. `--restart=Never` ensures a plain Pod is created (not a Deployment). `-n` selects the namespace.

[kubectl run reference](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubectl/kubectl-commands#run)

front: Generate the YAML for a pod (image `nginx`, name `nginx`) **without creating it**, and save it to `pod.yaml`
back:
```bash
kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --restart=Never --dry-run=client -o yaml > pod.yaml
```

`--dry-run=client -o yaml` prints the manifest without submitting it to the cluster — the standard way to scaffold YAML in the exam. To include a command in the generated spec, append `--command -- env`. You can also pipe straight to creation: `... -o yaml | kubectl create -n mynamespace -f -`.

[kubectl conventions (--dry-run)](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/conventions/)

front: Create a resource from a YAML manifest file `pod.yaml`
back:
```bash
kubectl create -f pod.yaml
```

Submits the manifest to the cluster. `kubectl apply -f pod.yaml` also works and is idempotent (creates or updates).

[kubectl Quick Reference](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/quick-reference/)

front: Create a `busybox` pod that runs the command `env`
back:
```bash
kubectl run busybox --image=busybox --command --restart=Never -- env
```

`--command` makes everything after `--` the container's *command* (entrypoint) instead of its *args*. Add `-it --rm` to see the output directly and delete the pod afterwards, or check the output with `kubectl logs busybox`.

[Define a command and arguments for a container](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/inject-data-application/define-command-argument-container/)

front: Get the YAML for a new namespace called `myns` **without creating it**
back:
```bash
kubectl create namespace myns -o yaml --dry-run=client
```

Same `--dry-run=client -o yaml` pattern as with pods — works for almost every `kubectl create` sub-command.

[Namespaces](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/namespaces/)

front: Create the YAML for a ResourceQuota called `myrq` with hard limits of 1 CPU, 1G memory and 2 pods, **without creating it**
back:
```bash
kubectl create quota myrq --hard=cpu=1,memory=1G,pods=2 --dry-run=client -o yaml
```

`kubectl create quota` generates a ResourceQuota; `--hard` takes a comma-separated list of resource=quantity pairs.

[Resource Quotas](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/policy/resource-quotas/)

front: Get pods in **all** namespaces
back:
```bash
kubectl get po -A
```

`-A` is the short form of `--all-namespaces`. `po` is the short name for `pods`.

[kubectl Quick Reference](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/quick-reference/)

front: Create a pod with image `nginx` called `nginx`, exposing (declaring) container port 80
back:
```bash
kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --restart=Never --port=80
```

`--port` sets `containerPort: 80` in the pod spec. It documents the port the container listens on; it does not create a Service.

[kubectl run reference](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubectl/kubectl-commands#run)

front: Change a running pod's image to `nginx:1.24.0`
back:
```bash
kubectl set image pod/nginx nginx=nginx:1.24.0
```

Syntax: `kubectl set image POD/POD_NAME CONTAINER_NAME=IMAGE:TAG`. The container is killed and recreated with the new image as soon as it is pulled (the pod itself stays, its RESTARTS counter increments).

[Images](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/containers/images/)

front: Watch a pod's status changes live
back:
```bash
kubectl get po nginx -w
```

`-w` / `--watch` keeps the command running and prints a new line on every state change — useful to observe restarts and rollouts.

[kubectl Quick Reference](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/quick-reference/)

front: Extract a single field from a pod with JSONPath (e.g. its image, or its IP)
back:
```bash
kubectl get po nginx -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[].image}{"\n"}'
```

`-o jsonpath` extracts specific fields from an object. Another common use — get the pod IP:

```bash
kubectl get pod nginx -o jsonpath='{.status.podIP}'
```

[JSONPath support](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/jsonpath/)

front: List pods showing their IPs and nodes
back:
```bash
kubectl get po -o wide
```

`-o wide` adds extra columns: pod IP, node, nominated node, readiness gates.

[kubectl Quick Reference](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/quick-reference/)

front: Get information about a pod, including details about potential issues (e.g. pod hasn't started)
back:
```bash
kubectl describe po nginx
```

`describe` shows the pod's configuration, conditions and — most importantly — the **Events** section at the bottom, which reveals scheduling/pull/start problems.

[Debug Pods](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug/debug-application/debug-pods/)

front: Get a running pod's YAML
back:
```bash
kubectl get po nginx -o yaml
```

Prints the full live object, including `status`. Equivalent forms: `-oyaml`, `--output yaml`, `--output=yaml`.

[kubectl Quick Reference](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/quick-reference/)

front: Get a pod's logs
back:
```bash
kubectl logs nginx
```

Prints stdout/stderr of the pod's (single) container. Add `-f` to follow, `-c <container>` for multi-container pods.

[Logging Architecture](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/cluster-administration/logging/)

front: A pod crashed and restarted — get the logs of the **previous** container instance
back:
```bash
kubectl logs nginx -p
```

`-p` / `--previous` shows logs of the prior, terminated instance of the container — essential when debugging crash loops.

[Debug Running Pods](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug/debug-application/debug-running-pod/)

front: Execute an interactive shell on the `nginx` pod
back:
```bash
kubectl exec -it nginx -- /bin/sh
```

`-it` attaches an interactive terminal; everything after `--` is the command run inside the container.

[Get a Shell to a Running Container](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug/debug-application/get-shell-running-container/)

front: Run a **temporary** pod (interactive, deleted automatically when it exits) — e.g. a busybox to `wget` another pod's IP
back:
```bash
kubectl run busybox --image=busybox --rm -it --restart=Never -- wget -O- 10.1.1.131:80
```

`--rm` deletes the pod right after it exits; `-it` shows the output interactively. Ideal for one-off connectivity checks and throwaway commands, e.g. `-- /bin/sh -c 'echo hello world'`.

[kubectl run reference](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubectl/kubectl-commands#run)

front: Create a busybox pod that echoes 'hello world' and then exits, showing the output
back:
```bash
kubectl run busybox --image=busybox -it --restart=Never -- echo 'hello world'
```

Everything after `--` (without `--command`) becomes the container's *args*, passed to the image's default entrypoint. Also valid: `-- /bin/sh -c 'echo hello world'`.

[Define a command and arguments for a container](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/inject-data-application/define-command-argument-container/)

front: Create an `nginx` pod with the environment variable `var1=val1`
back:
```bash
kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --restart=Never --env=var1=val1
```

`--env=KEY=VALUE` can be repeated for multiple variables. Verify with `kubectl exec -it nginx -- sh -c 'echo $var1'` or `kubectl describe po nginx | grep val1`.

[Define Environment Variables for a Container](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/inject-data-application/define-environment-variable-container/)

front: Print all environment variables inside a running pod
back:
```bash
kubectl exec -it nginx -- env
```

Runs `env` inside the container. Handy to verify injected variables from `--env`, ConfigMaps or Secrets.

[Get a Shell to a Running Container](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug/debug-application/get-shell-running-container/)

front: Define a pod with **two** busybox containers, both running `echo hello; sleep 3600` (edit generated YAML)
back:
```bash
vi pod.yaml
```

Generate a single-container pod with `kubectl run ... --dry-run=client -o yaml`, then duplicate the entry under `spec.containers` and give it a **different name**:

```yaml
spec:
  containers:
  - args: [/bin/sh, -c, 'echo hello;sleep 3600']
    image: busybox
    name: busybox
  - args: [/bin/sh, -c, 'echo hello;sleep 3600']
    image: busybox
    name: busybox2
```

There is no imperative flag for multiple containers — you always edit the YAML.

[Pods with multiple containers](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/pods/#how-pods-manage-multiple-containers)

front: Run a command (e.g. `ls`) in a **specific container** of a multi-container pod (pod `busybox`, container `busybox2`)
back:
```bash
kubectl exec -it busybox -c busybox2 -- ls
```

`-c` / `--container` selects the container; without it kubectl uses the first container (or the one annotated as default). Same flag works for an interactive shell (`-- /bin/sh`) and for `kubectl logs`.

[Get a Shell to a Running Container](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug/debug-application/get-shell-running-container/)

front: Share an `emptyDir` volume between containers of a pod — define the volume and mount it (e.g. at `/usr/share/nginx/html`) (edit generated YAML)
back:
```bash
vi pod-init.yaml
```

Declare the volume at `spec.volumes` and mount it via `volumeMounts` in every container that needs it (each container can use a different `mountPath`):

```yaml
spec:
  containers:
  - image: nginx
    name: nginx
    volumeMounts:
    - name: vol
      mountPath: /usr/share/nginx/html
  volumes:
  - name: vol
    emptyDir: {}
```

`emptyDir` starts empty, lives as long as the pod, and is the standard way to share files between containers of the same pod.

[emptyDir volumes](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/volumes/#emptydir)

front: Add a busybox **init container** that runs `echo "Test" > /work-dir/index.html` with a volume mounted at `/work-dir` (edit generated YAML)
back:
```bash
vi pod-init.yaml
```

Init containers go under `spec.initContainers` (same schema as regular containers) and run to completion, in order, **before** the app containers start:

```yaml
spec:
  initContainers:
  - args: [/bin/sh, -c, 'echo "Test" > /work-dir/index.html']
    image: busybox
    name: box
    volumeMounts:
    - name: vol
      mountPath: /work-dir
```

Combined with a shared `emptyDir` volume, this is the classic pattern for pre-populating content (e.g. an nginx web root).

[Init Containers](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/pods/init-containers/)
